text: CARACENILLA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 26 m. W of Cuenca, near the l. bank of the Huete.
CARACHE, a town of Venezuela, 20 m. NNE of Truxillo, on a river of the same name, an affluent of the Motatan.

CARACHE (SIERRA DEL), a mountain-ridge of Spain, which runs along the SE district of the prov. of Albacete, extending from the Segura on the SW, and terminates at St. Felipe in Valencia, where it unites with the Sierra de Bujarron.

CARACHIS, an aboriginal tribe of New Grenada in Assuay, who inhabit the district between the Putumayo or Ica, and the Amazon. CARACOL, BABAHOGO, or OXIBA, a river of New Grenada, in the prov. of Guayaquil, which takes its rise on the N side of Mount Carguairazo, runs SSW, and falls into Lake Samboramban, 25 m N of Guayaquil, after a course of 90 m., the greater part of which is navigable.
CARACOLES POINT, a promontory of New Grenada, on the E side of the bay of Panama, in N lat. 7° 45'.
CARACOLLA, a town of Bolivia, in the prov. and 30 m. SSE of Sicasica, on a tributary of the Desaguadero.

CARACU, or Acaracu, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, at the embouchure of the river of the same name, in S lat. 2° 50'. It comprises a superficies-extending inland to the lofty range of the Acaracu-of about 25 m., and contains a pop. of 2,500. Cotton and millet are extensively cultivated in the locality, and the produce of the adjacent fisheries forms an important article of export.-The river C. rises in the N declivity of the Serra Tatajuba, passes Guixeramobi, and Januaria, where it receives the Jahibara, and becomes navigable. From Sobral it directs its course to the N, and after a total course Jericoa-Coara. It shallows considerably during the dry season; and a sand bar at its embouchure renders it difficult of navigation.
CARACUEL, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 13 m. SSW of Ciudad-Real, La Manche, and 1 m. S of Corral-de-Calatrava.
CARADEC (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Côtes-du-Nord, cant. and 4 m. WNW of Loudeac, on the r. bank of the Ouste. Pop. 2,169.
CARADEC-TREGOMEL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Guémené. Pop. 1,166.
CARA-DEMELGE, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Thessaly, 17 m. SSE of Larissa.
CARADJAH, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, in the sanjak and 25 m. SE of Uskiep.

CARADJOVA, a mountain of Turkey in Europe, on the confines of the sanjaks of Ghiustendil and Salonica. It gives rise to a river of the same name, which runs first ESE, passes Moglena, and thence bending S, unites with the Vistriza 10 m. WNW of the entrance of that river into Lake Jenidja.
CARAETAYU MOUNTAINS, a range in British Guayana, between the parallels of 2° and 3° N, and intersected by the meridian of 58° W. They rise at least 1,000 ft. above the surrounding plain.—Schomburgk.
CARAGA, a fortified town of the island of Mindanao, cap. of a prov. of the same name, on a bay of the E coast, 100 m. E of Mindanao. The chief production of the prov. is sago. Pop. 19,183.
CARAGH, a parish in co. Kildare, 2½ m. NW of Naas. Area 3,733 acres. Pop. 921.

CARAGLIO, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento, in the prov. and 8 m. WNW of Coni, on the l. bank of the Grana. Pop. 6,000. It contains a communal college, and has several silk spinningmills and manufactories. Fairs are held in this town five times a-year.
CARAGOLA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, on the l. bank of the Ganges, 35 m. NW of Rajamal, and 110 m. NNW of Murshedabad.
CARAHA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Santa- Catherina, which runs near Lages, and falls into the Iguacu, an affluent of the Parana. Grinding-stone of good quality is found in its vicinity.
CARAHIAHI, an aboriginal tribe of Brazilian Guayana, who inhabit the town of Moura, on the r. bank of the Rio Negro.
CARAHIBAS, a small town of Brazil, in the prov. of Maranhao, on the l. bank of the Parnahiba, and near Conceição.
CARAHIPE, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Espirito-Santo, district of Victoria, near the mouth of the river of the same name. It is inhabited by Indians of the Goitacazes tribe.
CARA-ISMAK, a river of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, which forms the ontlet of Lake Jenidja, and unites with the Vardar, 3 m. NW of the entrance of that river into the gulf of Salonica.
CARAIANI, or Cojani, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, in the sanjak and 69 m. SE of Monastir, on an affluent of the Indje Cara-Su.
CARAJAS, a general name for several tribes of aborigines who inhabit the banks of the Araguaia in Brazil,
CARAJATAS, a town of the island of Cuba, on the N coast, 130 m. ESE of Havannah.
CARAKAL, or KARAKAL, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Little Wallachia, 30 m. SE of Krajova, in a valley to the S of the Teslui. It contains 3 Greek churches.

CARAMAN, a canton, com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Villefranche. -The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,373. | the coast, 70 m. SSW of Valdovia. CAR of 125 m., falls into the Atlantic, 15 m. ESE of j -The com., 9 m. N of Villefranche, had a pop. of 2,425 in 1841.

CARAMAN, a town of Asiatic Turkey, the cap. of the pash. of Caramania, situated in a spacious valley at the S extremity of the vast plain of Iconium, at the foot of the lofty Bedlerin-Dagh, a branch of Mount Taurus, 18 hours from Erekli, and the same distance in time from Iconium. It covers a large area, though it consists of only 1,100 houses, 1,000 of which are occupied by Turks, and the rest by Armenians. The houses are low, and almost all are built of clay, or of bricks dried in the sun. Some coarse woollen cloths are manufactured here, and others of wool and cotton mixed. A considerable quantity of wool is brought from the neighbouring mountains, also goat and sheep skins, wax, scammony, and a kind of lichen used in the Levant in preparing morocco. A considerable trade is carried on with Smyrna, Cæsarea, and other towns of Asia Minor. This town is called Larenda in the public acts of the Turks; but the ruins of Larenda, which exhibit many sculptures and inscriptions, are to be seen at 4 or 5 m. distance.—Also a town of Syria, in the pash. of Aleppo, on the W side of the lake of Antioch, 12 m. N of Antioch, and 18 m. ESE of Alexandretta.

CARAMANIA, or KARAMANIA, an extensive pashalik of Asiatic Turkey; bounded on the W and N by Anadolia; on the NE by Sivas; on the W by Sivas and Marash; on the S by Adana. It comprises the sanjaks of Konieh, Beyshehr, Akshehr, Akserai, Kirshehr, Nikde, and Kaisariah. Its pop. is roughly estimated at 1,000,000. The rivers are in general narrow, shallow, and unfit for navigation; but the country is intersected by numerous streams, and contains several lakes which are well-stored with fish. A large portion of the prov. is mountainous, and covered by woods of oaks, and some species of pines rising 100 ft. in height. The vine and fig here exhibit the most luxuriant vegetation; and the laurel, myrtle, and clematis, together with innumerable odoriferous shrubs, flourish in profusion. Copious harvests are reaped from a rich deep soil; and during the peninsular war, contributed to supply the wants of the British troops stationed in the Mediterranean. The plant from which opium is obtained is extensively cultivated. Among the principal cities of this pash. known to Europeans are Konieh, Aphium or Carahissar, Caraman, and Kesariah. Throughout the province, the houses are constructed of earth, or of bricks baked in the sun. Many of these are miserable dwellings; but the climate is so genial that the Caramanians can pass three quarters of the year under the shade of the trees. The villages of the Turcoman shepherds are composed of neat huts covered with skins; others may be seen elevated on posts 9 or 10 ft. above the level of the ground, and resembling birds' nests more than habitations of men. Some of the inhabitants are mere lawless banditti; and pirates frequently shelter themselves behind the promontories of the Adana coast, whence they issue forth to commit their depredations on defenceless vessels. Several celebrated cities and extensive ruins, together with numerous monuments of antiquity, exist in C .- The name Caramania, given to it by Europeans, is unknown to the inhabitants. In the middle ages, however, a kingdom called Caramán-ili existed here. See articles Asia Minor and ANATOLIA.
CARAMANICO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Citra, 8 m. N of Sulmona, on a flank of Monte Majella [alt. 1,597 ft.]. Pop. 3,528. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.

CARAMAVIDAMO, a small town of Chile, on recensor and and a recensor and a recensor of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of the contract of
CARAMEL, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Coimbatur, 18 m. NW of Carun.
CARAMINAL, a town of Spain, in Gallicia, in the prov. of La Corogne, at the mouth of the Barbanza.

CARAMNASSA, a small river of Hindostan, rising near Bijagur, in N lat. 24° 37', and falling into the Ganges near Buxar, in N lat. 25° 35', forming the boundary throughout its course between Bahar and Allahabad. Contact with its waters is dreaded by the Hindus, as destroying the efficacy of all former acts of piety. Hence its name Carma nasa, i. e., 'Destroyer of pious deeds.'
CARAMPUNDY, a small town of Hindostan, in the N Circars, 55 m. W by N of Guntur.
CARAMUZIN, a mountain in British Guayana, on the l. bank of the Caphicouin, in N lat. 1° 23', W long. 57° 47'. It has an alt. of about 2,000 ft. above the river, or 2,850 above the sea.—Schomburgk.
CARANDA, a small river of Brazil, in the prov. of Mato-Grosso, flowing into the Cuiaba on the l. bank.
CARANDAGA, a small island in that part of the Eastern Indian ocean which is called the sea of Mindoro, in N lat. 10° 25'.

CARANG-ASSOM, a town and district on the E coast of the island of Bali, in the straits of Lombok. It is the principal town of the island, and is situated in a populous and fertile territory, at the base of the peak of Bali.

CARANGAS, a district of Bolivia, bounded on the N by the prov. of Pacages; E by Paria; S by Lipes; and W by Arica. It is 36 leagues in length from N to S; and 30 wide. Its climate is cold, and it has only 1,100 inhabitants. It contains several rich silver mines.
CARANGOLA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, comarca of Campos, an affluent of the Muriaré.
CARANJA, an island in the Indian ocean, near the Concan, 5 m. E of Bombay, in N lat. 86° 56'.
CARANTEC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Taulé. Pop. 1,231.
CARANTILLY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Marigny, 8 m. SW of Saint-Lo. Pop. 1,442.
CARAPACO, a town of Bolivia, in the prov. of Atacama, on the coast, 45 m. NNW of Atacama.
CARAPAPURI, a river of Brazilian Guayana, inosculating with the Avagnari, and flowing into Penzon bay.

CARAPELLA, a river of Naples, which rises in Monte Irpino in Principato Ultra, traverses the Tavoliere di Puglia, and flows into the Cervaro near its embouchure, after a course of 60 m.—Also a small town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra, 15 m. E by S of Aquila.
CARAPIBUS, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, comarca of Campos, on the margin of a lake of the same name.
CARAPINA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Espirito-Santo, district of the city of Victoria.
CARARA. See CARRARA.
CARARE (NUEVO), a river of New Grenada, flowing into the Magdalena, on the r. bank, in N lat. 6° 50', after a NNW course of 120 m.
CARARI, a town of Bolivia, 32 m. NW of Cochabamba.
CARASCAL, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. of Caceres, 10 m. NNE of Truxillo.
CARASCHE, one of the Bissagos group, off the W coast of Africa, in 11° 15' N lat.

CARATASKA, or Cartago Lake, a large lagune on the Musqueto coast of Guatemala, intersected by the parallel of 15° 25' N, and the meridian of 84° W. II. CAR It is upwards of 40 m. long, and 10 m. in breadth, and receives a river of the same name on its SW extremity. A fine island called Turgin, well-suited for breeding cattle, and consisting of alternate savanna and pine ridge, occupies its centre.
CARATE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, near the river Lambro, 14 m. N of Milan. Pop. 1,800.
CARAU'BA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Parahiba, in the district of Villa-Real-de-São-João.
CARAUBAL, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Maranhao, on the Itapicuru-Mirim.
CARAVACA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Murcia, on the borders of New Castile, in a hilly district, 40 m. WNW of Murcia, on a river of the same name, an affluent of the Segura.
CARAVAES, a small island in the Philippine group, in the Indian ocean, off the NW point of Panay.

CARAVAGGIO, a small town of Venetian Lombardy, between Brescia and Milan, 10 m. N of Crema. Pop. 5,000. It claims to be the birth-place of M. A. Amerighi, commonly called Il Caravaggio. CARAVAN,-from kar-revan, 'travelling merchandise,'-the name given in the East to a company of merchants or pilgrims who travel through the deserts in a body, in order to afford mutual protection against the attacks of the Arabs and robbers by whom they are infested.—A very extensive and lucrative commerce has from time immemorial been carried on between Central Africa, and the maritime states of Marocco, Tunis. Algiers, Tripoli, and Egypt, by means of kafilahs, coffas, or akabahs, or accumulated caravans. These caravans consist of several hundred loaded camels, attended by Arabs who let them at a low rate to the merchants; and they generally cross the great desert of Sahara between the months of September and April. The line of their march is extremely irregular, as they are obliged to turn out of their direct route to fall in with the fertile and cultivated spots called oases: The caravans remain at each of these oases about seven days; and after being supplied with water, and feeding and refreshing their camels, they proceed to another spot of the same kind, lying in or near the line of route. "The akkabahs which cross the desert," says Mr. Jackson, "may be compared to our fleets of merchant-vessels under convoy; the stata, or convoy of the desert, being two or more Arabs belonging to the tribe through whose territory the caravan passes; thus in passing the territory of Woled Abussebah, they are accompanied by two Sebayhees, a people of that country, who, on reaching the confines of the territory of Woled Deleim, receive a remuneration, and return, delivering them to the protection of two chiefs of Woled Deleim; these again conduct them to the confines of the territory of the Mograffa Arabs; and so on, till they reach Timbuctu. Any assault made against the akkabah during this journey is considered as an insult to the whole clan to which the convoy belongs, and for which they never fail to take ample revenge. Besides these grand accumulated caravans, there are others which cross the desert on any emergency, without a stata, or guard of soldiers; but this is a perilous expedition, and they are often plundered near the northern confines of the desert. In the year 1798, an akkabah consisting of 2,000 camels loaded with Sudanic produce, together with 700 slaves, was plundered and dispersed, and many were killed." The manners and customs of the Mahommedan merchants during these long and dangerous journeys are extremely simple. Prohibited by their religion from the use of intoxicating liquors, their only nourishment consists of a few dates and a draft of water; and when their journey is only for a few weeks, a little barley meal and cold water is their only food. The day's journey is generally terminated about 4 o'clock; and after pitching their tents, and saying their prayers, they prepare their supper; as soon as it is ready, they seat themselves round in a circle; and thereafter talk till they are overcome by sleep. At break of day they again proceed upon their journey. In the course of these journeys, the hot winds called shume are often so violent as to evaporate all the water carried in skins; and Mr. Jackson was informed by the Arabs and the people of Sudan, that on these occasions, 500 dollars have been given for a draft of water. A caravan proceeding from Timbuctu to Tafilet in the year 1805, was disappointed at not finding water at one of the oases, and the whole party, consisting of 2,000 men and 1,800 camels, perished of thirst. The dangers arising from the drifting of the sand, are likewise very considerable. When the loose sand is drifted along the plains by the shume, the kafilahs are obliged suddenly to strike their tents, otherwise they would be all buried in the overwhelming torrent. On these occasions the desert has the appearance of a sea, as the drifting-sand has a striking resemblance to the waves of the ocean. Hence the desert is emphatically called by the Arabs 'a sea without water.' A good descriptive picture of a caravan is given by Chateaubriand, in his Travels in Greece and Palestine. "It was midnight," says he, "when we arrived at the khan of Menemen. I perceived at a distance a great num ber of scattered lights: it was a caravan making a halt. On a nearer approach, I distinguished camels, some lying, others standing; some with their loads, others relieved from their burdens. Horses and asses without bridles, were eating barley out of leather buckets; some of the men were still on horseback, and the women, veiled, had not alighted from their dromedaries. Turkish merchants were seated cross-legged on carpets, in groups round the fires, at which the slaves were busily employed in dressing pilau. Other travellers were smoking their pipes at the door of the khan, chewing opium, and listening to stories. Here were people burning coffee in iron pots; there hucksters went about from fire to fire, offering cakes, fruits, and poultry for sale. Singers were amusing the crowd; imans were performing their ablutions, prostrating themselves, rising again, and invoking the prophet; and the camel-drivers lay snoring on the ground. The place was strewed with packages, bags of cotton, and couffs of rice. All these objects now distinct, now confused and enveloped in a half shade, exhibited a genuine scene of the Arabian Nights." The vast deserts of Central Africa are traversed by 6 different lines of commercial communication, by means of caravans or kafilahs, generally consisting of from 1,800 to 2,000 camels; five of these proceed from S to N, and one from E to W. At the point of arrival and departure there are numerous lines communicating with places in the deserts or on the coast, which are traversed by gazzies or small caravans with camels, by mules, asses, men and women. These 6 lines of communication are as follows: 1st. The kafilah of Darfur from S to N. From Qorbi, on the frontiers of the valley of the Nile, to Siout in Upper Egypt, via El Karged, being a 31 days' journey. The caravans at this place formerly started every 6 months, but now only once in 15 months. They consist of from 1,000 to 1,100 camels, carrying brass, hides, gums and drugs; and of from 1,500 to 1,800 slaves. 2d. The kafilahs of Ouaday and Dar Saleh, to the number of 5 annually, pursue a tract which lies between the lines of march followed by the caravans of Darfur and those which traverse Fezzan. The caravans consist of about 1,000 camels, which carry principally brass, hides, drugs, and provisions for the 2,000 or 2,500 slaves conveyed by them to the merchants of Aoudgellah, who sell them in Egypt. These caravans, in their new direction, which has made it necessary to dig wells, to build caravanseries, magazines, &c., have been established posterior to the European prohibition of the slave trade on the coast of Guinea. About 50 days are occupied in reaching Aoudgellah. 3d. The kafilahs of Bornu assemble on the Yaouri and Lake Tchad. There are two annually. They proceed directly N, arriving at Morzuk via Bilmah and the oasis of the Tibbus. Here they divide into several smaller caravans or gazzies. Those intended for the NW proceed by the mountains of the Ghyaats to Ghadamés, Touzer, Tunis, the island of Gerba and Tripoli. Those to the N go by two routes to Tripoli via Sognâ. The caravans of Aoudgellah and Egypt proceed to the NE. The kafilahs consist of 2,000 camels richly laden. They escort from 1,800 to 2,000 slaves, and are 70 days in arriving at Morzuk. 4th. The caravans of Haousâ form at Aghadez, the oasis of the Tourics. They proceed by the oasis of the Tuats to Ghadames, whence they separate into the different routes leading to the ports and cities on the coast of the states of Barbary. At Temymouw, the metropolis of the oasis of the Tuats, they meet with a portion of the kafilah of Timbuctu, intended for the merchants of Ghadames, Tunis, Tripoli, and even of Constantin. This caravan of 2,000 camels, with from 1,500 to 1,800 slaves, occupies from 58 to 60 days between Aghads and Ghadamés. There are at least two in a year. 5th. The great kafilah of Meli, Oussolo, and Ouanqarah, assembles at Timbuctu and El-Arxouâm. It proceeds NW to Tafilet, and thence to the ports of the empire of Marocco, to the ocean, to Fez, Tangier, Tetuan, Tlémícen, and Oran. It consists of from 1,800 to 2,000 camels, and 700 or 800 slaves. This caravan is richly laden, being almost entirely intended for the Moorish merchants of Marocco. It occupies from 78 to 80 days in arriving at Fez from Timbuctu; and if there are many slaves, 90 days. There are 3 or 4 annually. 6th. The kafilah of pilgrims from Marocco formerly traversed the limits of the desert from W to E, outside the declivities S of the Atlas. During the latter period of the rule of Algiers, it entered this country above the desert of Angad, arrived by a gorge of the Ouanascherischs in the valley of the Setif and the lake of Titori, and thence proceeded to Constantin and Tunis. It followed the borders of the gulf of Cabis to Tripoli, and thence entered the deserts of Bargah and Libya. On arriving at Cairo, this caravan having been increased on the road, consisted of from 2,500 to 3,000 camels, and included as many as 3,000 to 4,000 men, women, and children. This kafilah employed a whole year in going to Mecca, paying devotions there, and in returning. The trade of the E part of the deserts is carried on with Bengazi, Derna, Sliten, Porto-Magra, and Alexandria. All the force of these kafilahs is concentrated on the empire of Marocco. The inhabitants of Erouaghah and Mozab, tributaries of Algiers, carry on a direct trade with the oases of the Truats. Before the war between Tunis and Algiers in 1782, and the plague of Tunis in 1783, a caravan left Constantina monthly for Tunis, carrying goods to a value of from 100,000 to 120,000 Spanish piastres, and occupying 18 days on the journey. This caravan has been resumed, and daily increases in importance. - Chateaubriand's Travels in Greece, Palestine, Egypt, and Barbary. Volney's Voyage CAR en Syrie et en Egypte.-Russel's History of Aleppo.-Pococke's Description of Egypt.—Maillet Description de l'Egypte, Paris, 1740 -Rennel, Phil. Trans. vol. lxxxi. - Jackson's Account of Marocco -Browne's Travels in Africa. - Montveran's Etudes Statistiques sur l'Afrique Boréale.—See also the articles Arabia, Asia, Barbart, CAIRO, MAROCCO.

CARAVA'NSERA', or CARAVANSERA'I, the name given in the East to large inns or public buildings for accommodating the merchants, pilgrims, and travellers, that accompany the caravans. The caravanseras have generally been confounded with the khans; but the former, particularly about the time of their origin, were erected under the impulse of religious duty, in desert places, at a distance from large towns, to afford shelter and accommodation to travellers and caravans; while the khans, which were generally built in towns, were used by foreign merchants, not only as a lodging-bouse, but as a magazine for their goods. The word caravansera, however, is the general name adopted in Asia for all these kinds of buildings. It is used not only in Turkey, but also in Persia and the Mogul country, while the word khan is more particularly used in Turkey. Neither beds, kitchen, nor provisions, are attached to these buildings; and the traveller must either carry along with him every thing that he wants, or purchase them after his arrival. The caravanseras, as well as the khans, are generally huge square buildings, being one or two stories high above the ground floor. The interior façades,—which form a spacious court or hall in the middle, -resemble the cloisters of convents. The magazines are in the ground floors, while the chambers for travellers are in the upper stories. A reservoir of water, in which the camels and other beasts of burden quench their thirst, is placed in the middle of the court. The permission to build a caravansera has always been considered in Turkey as a mark of distinction. At one time, however, this privilege was bestowed only upon the mothers and sisters of the sultans, and upon the viziers and bashaws who had gained three victories over the Christians. For an account of several of the caravanseras in Persia, see Morier's Journey through Persia, Armenia, and Asia Minor, and Jackson's Account of Marocco.
CARAVANSERAI, a village of Greece, on the coast of the gulf of Arta, N of Lake Ambracia.
CARAVELLA (POINT), a cape on the NE coast of Martinico, in N lat. 14° 48′ 28", W long. 60° 56' 12".

CARAVELLAS, a district and seaport of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, near the N side of a bay of the same name, about 5 m. from the coast, and 10 m. N of the Peruhipe, in S lat. 17° 42'. Mandioc is largely cultivated in the district, which has a pop. of about 5,000.—The C. river, which falls into the bay, communicates with the Peruhipe, affording a passage from Vicoza. It is 2 m. broad at its mouth, but its bar cannot be passed by vessels drawing above 12 ft.
CARAVINO, a town of Sardinia, in Piedmont, 9 m. SE of Ivrea. Pop. 1,500.
CARAWANG (CAPE), a promontory on the N coast of the island of Java, in E long. 107° 15'.
CARBAJALES, or Carvajales, a small town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 12 m. NW of Zamora.
CARBAJALES-DE-LA-ENCOMIENDA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. of Valladolid, 34 m. WNW of Benaventa. Pop. 2,000.
CARBAJOSA, a town of Spain, in Leon, near the r. bank of the Duro, 12 m. W of Zamora.
CARBALLO (SAN JUAN DE), a village of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 10 m. from Corunna. Pop. 365. It is of great antiquity, and is celebrated for its mineral baths.
CARBET (LE), a town of the island of Martinico, situated on the NW coast, with a good port.
CARBINI, a town of Corsica, 25 m. SE of Ajaccio. Pop. 1,600.
CARBON (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of Algiers, at the entrance of the gulf of Bugia, 12 m. from Cape Sigli, and 30 m. from Point Jijeli.

CARBONARA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Bari, 2 m. SSE of Bari. Pop. 2,451.—Also a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, 20 m. SE of Mantua, on the r. bank of the Po. Pop. 2,000. -Also a town of Naples, in Principato Ultra, 15 m. ENE of San-Angelo de' Lombardi. Pop. 2,886.
CARBONARA (CAPE), a promontory on the SE coast of Sardinia, near the gulf of Cagliari, in N lat. 39° 6° 45".

CARBON-BLANC, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bordeaux, on the r. bank of the Garonne.—The cant. comprises 17 com.—The com. is 5 m. NE of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,900.
CARBONE, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 32 m. WSW of Tursi.
CARBONIERE, an island off the coast of Newfoundland, in Conception bay, in N lat. 47° 44′ 30", W long. 53° 10′ 40″.
CARBONNE, a canton and com. of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Muret. The cant. comprises 10 com. The com. is on the r. bank of the Garonne, 12 m. SW of Muret. Pop. 1,981.
CARBROOKE, a parish in Norfolk, 2 m. NW of Walton. Area 3,020 acres. Pop. 807.
CARBURTON, a chapelry in the p. of Edwinstow, in Nottinghamshire, 4 m. SSE of Worksop. Pop. 193.
CARCAGENTE, a town of Spain, in Valencia, near the r. bank of the Zucar, 19 m. N of San Felipe. It carries on a trade in silk, oranges, and other fruits. Pop. 8,300.
CARCAGUY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Tilly-sur-Seulles. Pop. 557.
CARCAINERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Querigut, 30 m. SE of Foix. Pop. 243.

CARCANS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of St. Laurent-de-Madoc, near a lagune of the same name, which runs parallel to the coast, and communicates with the estuary of the Levre. Pop. 1,005.
CARCARA, a town of Piedmont, on the Bormida, 12 m. E of Ceva.
CARCARANAL, or TERCERO, a river of Buenos Ayres, which rises in the mountains to the W of Cordova, and flows E to the Parana. It might be rendered navigable at a moderate expense.
CARCASIN, a river of Texas, flowing into the gulf of Mexico 27 m. E by N of the mouth of the Sabine. Close to the coast it forms a lake, which at high water is connected with the sea.
CARCASS ISLAND, one of the W Falkland group. It has a high double peak in its centre, and rabbits are abundant upon it.

CARCASSONNE, an ancient city of France, the cap. of the dep. of Aude, in N lat. 43° 12' 55", E long. 2° 20′ 46", 495 m. by road S of Paris, 30 m. W of Narbonne, on the river Aude, at an alt. of 485 ft. above sea-level. The river divides it into an upper and a lower town. The former, or 'city' as it is called, is on the r. bank of the river, and has a prevailing air of antiquity about it. The lower and newer town occupies a lower site on the opposite bank of the river, and is neatly built. A bridge of 10 arches maintains the communication between the two towns. The fortifications of the lower town have given place to gardens and boulevards; but the double walls of the old town still surround it. The cathedral of Saint-Nazaire, a curious edifice of the 11th cent., stands in the middle of the old town. The tower of the church of Saint-Vincent, in the new town, is said to be on the meridian line of Paris. The hotel de ville, and the halles or covered market-places, are also worthy of notice in the lower town. C. is the seat of an ancient bishopric, of a diocesan seminary, and tribunals of primary jurisdiction and commerce. It has a public library of 20,000 vols., a primary normal school, and a communal college. Its pop. in 1789 was 12,064; in 1836, 18,907; in 1846, 18,537. It is one of the chief seats of the fine woollen manufactures of France. Its black kerseymeres are excellent; and red and bright coloured cloths are exten- 2,482, and the tonnage 183,480. The number of CAR sively manufactured here for the Mediterranean markets. It has also fabrics of hosiery, linen, soap, paper, leather, and nails; and its commerce is much facilitated by a branch of the Canal du Midi.—C. was the Carcasum, Volcarum-Tectosagum, and Carcassio of early times. Cæsar speaks of it as one of the towns of Gallia Ulterior. The Visigoths wrested it from the Romans, and were in their turn dispossessed of it by the Saracens in 724. The Franks gave to it a line of dukes, the last of whom, Raymond-Trencavel II., ceded his estates, in 1247, to Philip-Augustus.—The painter Gamelin, Meric, president of the corps legislatif under Napoleon, and the revolutionist Fabre d'Eglantine, president of the Tribunat, were natives of C. The arrond. of C. has an area of 192,612 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 94,329; in 1846, 95,680. It comprises 11 cantons: viz., Alzonne, Capendu, C., Conques, Lagrasse, Mas - Cabardes, Montreal, Mouthoumet, Peyriac-Minervois, Saissac, and Tuchan.—The cant. of C. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 21,057.
CARCASTILLO, a town of Spain, in Navarre, 9 m. S of Sanguesa, on the l. bank of the Aragon.
CARCAVELLOS, a village of Portugal, in Estremadura, 12 m. W of Lisbon, near the coast. Pop. 350. It is celebrated for its wine.
CARCEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Tartas. Pop. 484.
CARCES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Cotignac, 7 m. NE of Brignoles. Pop. 2,217.
CARCHUNA, or SACRATIF (CAPE), a promontory of Spain, on the coast of Andalusia, in N lat. 36° 4', W long. 2° 27′ 15″.
CAR-COLSTON, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 8 m. S of Southwell. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 276.
CARCORA, a village on the E coast of the gulf of Sidra, in N lat. 31° 30', E long. 20°.
CARDAILLAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of La-Capelle-Marival, 4 m. NNW of Figeac. Pop. 1,300.
CARDANGAN, a parish in co. Tipperary, adjoining the town of Tipperary. Area 3,905 acres. Pop. 3,088.
CARDEN, a township in the p. of Tilston, in Cheshire, 41 m. NNW of Malpas. Pop. 233.

CARDENAS, a town on the N coast of Cuba, 43 m. E of Matanzas. Pop. 1,200. It has a small shallow harbour. General Lopez, with a part of his buccaneering expedition against Cuba, having sailed from New Orleans to the coast of Yucatan, on the 7th of May 1850, landed at this point of Cuba on the 17th, and compelled the garrison to surrender, but was checked in his advance upon Matanzas, and with difficulty effected his escape to New Orleans.
CARDESTON, a parish in Salop, 64 m. W of Shrewsbury. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 372.

CARDIFF, a borough in the co. of Glamorgan, 25 m. W of Bristol; 21 m. S by E of Merthyr-Tydvil; 160 m. W of London. The parliamentary and municipal boundaries are co-extensive, and extend nearly 3 m. from N to S, and upwards of 1 m. from E to W. The town lies on the E bank of the Taff, over which there is a handsome bridge of 5 arches leading to Swansea. The principal streets are paved, and lighted with gas. The town is thriving, and has a considerable trade. The export-trade is principally carried on with the port of Bristol; and consists of iron, tin, oats, barley, coal, butter, and poultry. By means of the canal from Pennarth to Merthyr-Tydvil, C. has become the connecting link between the extensive iron-works carried on there and the English market: it is in fact the port of Merthyr-Tydvil. In 1832, the number of vessels entering this port was sailing vessels registered as belonging to the port of C. on 1st January, 1848, was 21 above 50 tons, and 42 below 50 tons. Total tonnage 6,347 tons. There were also 7 steam-vessels = 179 tons. The number of sailing vessels that entered the port coastwise in <math>1848</math> was <math>838 = 24,293</math> tons, and that cleared outwards, <math>6,195 = 390,671</math> tons. The number of steamers that entered and cleared was 327 = 35,766 tons. The number that entered from the colonies in 1848 was 67 British <math>= 10,857</math> tons, and 17 Foreign <math>= 1,476</math> tons. The number that cleared outwards was 124 British <math>= 25,566</math> tons; Foreign <math>24 = 5,080</math> tons. The number that entered from Foreign ports in 1848 was 87 British = 13,572 tons, and 142 Foreign = 14,553 tons; that cleared outwards, 481 British = <math>75,921</math> tons, and <math>345</math> Foreign = <math>39,916</math> tons.—The Taff, which falls into the sea at C., forms a principal outlet for the mining districts of Glamorganshire; the produce of these mines has hitherto found its way to market through the Glamorganshire canal; but its sealock, constructed about forty years ago, has long been found inadequate to the demands for increased accommodation. In 1830, an act for constructing the Bute ship-canal was obtained. A straight open channel NNE and SSW, about 3 m. in length, leads from C. roads to the new sea-gates, which are 45 ft. wide, with a depth of 17 ft. water at neap, and 32 ft. at spring tide. On passing the sea-gate, vessels enter a capacious basin, calculated to accommodate vessels of great burden, and steamers. Beyond the lock, is the inner basin, which constitutes the grand feature of this work. It extends in a continuous line from the lock to near the town of C., 1,450 yards long, and 200 ft. wide, presenting an area of nearly 20 acres of water, capable of accommodating in perfect safety from 300 to 400 ships of all classes. Quays are built on each side two-thirds of its length, comprising nearly 6,000 ft., or more than 1 m. of wharfs, with ample space for warehouses, exclusive of the wharfs at the outer basin. To keep the channel free of deposit, a feeder from the river Taff supplies a reservoir 15 acres in extent adjoining the basin. This reservoir can be discharged at low water by means of powerful sluices, with cast-iron pipes 5 ft. in diameter, and by 10 sluices at the sea-gates, so as to deliver at the rate of 100,000 tons of water per hour. The limits of the port of C. are from the river Rumney to Nash point.—The South Wales railway passes through C., and steam-packets run between C. and Uphill, near the Bristol and Exeter railway.—The castle of C., which stands on a high mound of earth, in the midst of a large area, but was rapidly going to decay, has been gracefully restored by the Marquis of Bute, and retains much of its original grandeur.—C. is the cap. of the county, and joins with Cowbridge and Llan-Trisant in returning one member to parliament. In 1832 the parl. constituency of C. and its contributory boroughs was 687; in 1837, 590; in 1847, 990. The income of the borough in 1837 was £1,313, of which £803 arose from market - dues. The county - gaol and house-of-correction stand apart from other buildings at a convenient distance from the town. It is a modern edifice, opened in 1832, and cost about £12,000. Pop. in 1801, 1,870; in 1831, 6,137; in 1841, 10,077. —The C. poor-law union comprehends 44 parishes, with a pop. returned, in 1831, at 24,997; in 1841, at 32,552. The average annual expenditure on the poor of this district during the three years preceding the formation of the union was £11,039. Expenditure in 1838, £7,613; in 1847, £12,446. The annual value of assessed property in C. in 1842-3 was £42,188.
CARDIFFSTOWN, a parish in co. Kildare, 21 m. NNE of Naas. Area 703 acres. Pop. 35. CAR

CARDIGAN, a market, borough, union, and county-town in the hund. of Troedyraur, and county of Cardigan, on the river Teifi, 31 m. from its mouth, and 231 m. by road WNW of London. Pop. in 1801, 1,911; in 1831, 2,795; in 1841, 2,528; of the parl. borough, 2,925. The town lies within the parish of St. Mary, with which the old borough was co-extensive. The parl. boundary includes the hamlets of Bridge-end and Abbey in the parish of St. Dogmel, Pembroke. The new municipal boundary includes Bridge-end, but does not extend over the parish. C. occupies a steep hill on the N side of the Teifi; and consists of two principal streets containing some handsome houses. The river is crossed by a bridge of 7 arches leading to Milford. The church has a fine tower rising from the west end, and is upon the whole a stately as well as venerable edifice. C. in conjunction with Aberystwith, Adpar, and Lampeter, returns one member to parliament. The parliamentary constituency of C. and its contributory boroughs, according to the register of 1836, was 920; in 1847, 754. The coasting-trade of C. is considerable, employing upwards of 10,000 tons of shipping and 1,000 seamen. The limits of the port extend from 4 or 5 m. beyond Fishguard, on the coast of Pembroke, to a point on the coast NE of Aberaeron, and 24 m. from the mouth of the Teifi. With respect to C. itself, the bar is a very great impediment to the navigation, and the entrance to the harbour is extremely dangerous in winter. Vessels of 300 or 400 tons can come up to the town in spring-tides; but the general trade is confined to vessels of from 15 to 100 tons burthen About 300 vessels are registered as belonging to C. The imports are coal and culm; the latter from South Wales, the former principally from Liverpool, sometimes from South Wales or Staffordshire, and still more rarely from Gloucestershire; limestone from Pembrokeshire; and Memel and American timber. The exports are corn, chiefly oats, to Bristol and Liverpool, butter, and slates; which last may in fact be considered the staple article of the place. The land in the neighbourhood of this town is, in great measure, held in small lots, of the value of from £10 to £30 per annum, at from £1 10s. to £3 per acre. Further off, it is let in portions of from 40 to 130 acres, and at about 15s. an acre on the average. The tenures are generally from year to year; there are, however, a few small freeholders.-Of the castle, famous in Welsh history, there remain only two round towers connected by a curtain-wall venerably mantled with ivy .- C. gives the title of Earl to the noble family of Brudenel.—The C. poor-law union comprehends 26 parishes, with a pop. returned, in 1831, at 18,990; in 1841, at 19,901. Expenditure in 1836, £5,184; in 1846, £6,428.

CARDIGANSHIRE, a maritime county of S Wales; bounded by the counties of Montgomery and Merioneth on the N; by Radnor and Brecon on the E; by Carmarthen on the S; and on the W by the Irish sea. Its length of coast is 42 m. It is about 45 m. in length, and 35 m. in breadth; and contains an area of 675 sq. m., or 432,000 acres. It is divided into 5 hundreds, and 64 parishes; and is in the diocese of St. David's. The N and E districts are mountainous, with fertile valleys interspersed; towards the S and W the surface is level, and the soil productive. In the NE part of the county rises Plinlimmon, alt. 2,463 ft. Tregaron-Down has an alt. of 1,747 ft.; Talsarn, of 1,143 ft. In the vales, the predominating soil is peat, which yields, by the application of lime, rich crops both of grain and grass; on the higher grounds it is a sandy loam; the mountains are composed chiefly of clay slate.—The principal rivers are the Teify or Tivy, by which it is separated from the counties of Pembroke and Carmarthen; the Dovy, which divides it from Merioneth; the Ridol, the Ystwith, Aeron, Arth, Towy, and Cledon; besides innumerable rivulets, which, having their sources among the mountains, are often swollen into dangerous and impassable torrents. The agricultural produce of this county is comparatively trifling; little more than one-third of the surface is arable, and corn is often imported from other counties. Black cattle, sheep, and wool are its agricultural staples. Perhaps few counties in the kingdom are so rich in mineral treasures, while none derive less benefit from them. Silver, copper, and lead are found here. The immense fortune which Sir Hugh Middleton expended in bringing the New river to Islington, is well known to have been acquired in the silver and lead mines of Cwmsymlog; yet this, and many others in this county that have been successively opened, are now totally abandoned. Slates are largely quarried. There are no manufactures of importance. Gloves and coarse flannels are made, but to a small extent. The principal exports are butter, slates, and oats. - Besides Cardigan the county-town, this co. has the market-towns of Lampeter, Aberystwith, Tregarron, and part of Newcastle-in-Emlyn, and returns one member to parliament. The number of electors registered in 1837 was 1837, £20,681; in 1846, £20,293. The number of daily schools in this co. in 1835 was 92, attended by 4,082 children; of Sunday schools, 175, attended by 18,649 children. The annual value of real property in this co. in the year ending 5th April 1843 was £205,327.
CARDIGOS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 25 m. ENE of Thomar and 45 m. E of Leiria.
CARDIKE, or KARDIKE, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Albania, in the pash. and 25 m. SE of Janina, near the r. bank of the Aspro- Potamo.

CARDINALE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 25 m. SSW of Catanzaro, cant. and 5 m. SSE of Chiaravalle, on the r. bank of the Ancinale. Pop. 2,680. It possesses some manufactories of cloth.

CARDINGTON, a parish of Bedfordshire, 2 m. SSW of Willington, on the Ouse. Area 5,050 acres. Pop. in 1841, inclusive of the chapelry of East-Cotts, 1,466.—Also a parish of Salop, 4 m. ENE of Church- Stretton. Area 6,500 acres. Pop. 691.
CARDINHAM, a parish of Cornwall, 4 m. NE of Bedmin, intersected by the Fowey. Area 8,550 acres. Pop. in 1841, 802.
CARDIOTISSA, a small inhabited island of the Grecian archipelago, between the islands of Polycandro and Sikyno, in N lat. 36° 8', E long. 25° 1'.
CARDISTON, a parish of Salop. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 372.

CARDITELLO, a village of Naples, in the prov. I rounded by numerous villas, one of which was the CAR of Terra-di-Lavora, district and 9 m. W of Caserta, and 4 m. SSW of Capua. In the environs are a royal castle with a fine picture-gallery, a park, and a horse-breeding stud.
CARDITO, a village of Naples, in the prov. and 6 m. N of Naples. Pop. 2,730. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.

CARDIVA, an island of the Indian ocean, in the Maldive group, in N lat. 4° 55', E long. 73° 30'. Pop. 200. To the N and SW is a channel of the same name separating the islands of C. and Todou from Phaidi-Pholo and Horsburgh Atolls or groups of islets.
CARDON, an islet in the gulf of Panama, of volcanic origin.

CARDONA, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 45 m. NW of Barcelona, on the r. bank of the Cardoner - which is here crossed by a stonebridge -and near the confluence of the Bindassais, at an alt. of 1,394 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,366. It is surrounded by walls, and defended by a strong castle; and contains two parish-churches, a convent, an hospital, and two alms-houses. It has some manufactories of silk, cloth and other woollen fabrics, linen, and hardware.—In the environs is a remarkable mountain of mineral salt. The mines are situated about 3 m. to the E of the town, and resemble a huge stone quarry, 12 m. in circumf. The Cardonero, here not more than 20 ft. wide, flows through the midst of the mine on a bed of salt, and for many miles of its course, after issuing from the from Barcelona and Tarragona to the northern states Medina Celi, who pays a heavy tax for the monopoly of them into the royal treasury. The town of C., however, belongs to the Crown, and the numerous other unwrought beds of salt which lie around it. Every house in C. receives annually an arroba or 26 lbs. of salt gratis from the mine. The salt, which is of different colours, and of great hardness and transparency, is wrought like crystal into a variety of ornamental articles which do not liquefy in the dry air of Spain. The extraction of the salt is made in the open air, by horizontal cuts in the form of steps.

CARDONERO, a river of Spain, in Catalonia, which takes its rise in a branch of the Pyrenees, in the prov. of Lerida, 11 m. SE of Urgel, passes Cardona, where it receives the Bindassaes, and after a total course to the SSE of 45 m., unites with the Llobregat 3 m. S of Manresa.
CARDOSO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 35 m. NNW of Guadalaxara.
CARDOZO, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Sân Paulo, behind the bay of Cananea, 13 m. WNW of Bom-Abrigo, in N lat. 24° 58′ 45″.

CARDROSS, a parish in Dumbartonshire, stretching along the river Leven and the Clyde. Area 13,000 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,549; in 1841, 4,416. The great increase of the pop. is chiefly due to the introduction of extensive calico works into the district. The p. contains the villages of West Bridgend, Renton, Geilstone-Bridge, and Cardross.
CAREBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 5 m. SW of Bourn, on the Glen. Area 1,590 acres. Pop. in 1841, 73.
CAREGGI, a village of Tuscany, in the compart. and 2 m. from Florence, and 3 m. W of Fiesole. Pop. 443. It occupies a fine situation, and is surancient grand - ducal residence of the Medicis family.
CAREGGINE, a town of Modena, cap. of a com. in the district of Massa, 3 m. S of Camporgiano. Pop. 775. The wines of the environs are esteemed the best in the Garfagnana.

CAREIRAS, a river of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, which issues from the Serra Caldeiro, near the confines of the prov. of Algarve, runs ENE, and falls into the Guadiana after a course of about 33 m.
CAREMA, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 9 m. NNW of Ivrea, on the l. bank of the Doire Baltea.
CARENAGE (THE), or PORT CASTRIES, a bay and town of the island of St. Lucia, on the W coast, and in N lat. 13° 55'.—Carenage is a general name applied in the Antilles to a harbour.
CARENAS, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. of Zaragosa, on the r. bank of the Mesa, 9 m. SW of Calatayud.
CARENERO BAY, an indentation of the N coast of Venezuela, in the prov. of Caraccas, to the S of Cape Codera. It affords good careening accommodation-whence its name.
CARENNAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Vayrac, on the r. bank of the Dordogne, 21 m. NE of Gourdon. Pop. 1,084. Freestone is wrought in the environs.

CARENTAN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Manche, arrond. of Saint- Lo.—The cant. comprises 15 com., and in 1831 contained 12,244 inhabitants.—The town is situated in a marshy locality, about 8 m. from the mouth of the Tante, and 18 m. NNW of Saint-Lo. Pop. in 1841, 2,920. It is defended by fortifications and a strong castle, and has a small port. It contains manufactories of lace and calico, and possesses a considerable trade in cattle, horses, grain, hemp, lint, cider, butter, fish, wax, and honey.

CARENTOIR, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of Vannes. The cant. comprises 7 com., and in 1831 contained 11,530 inhabitants.—The town is 34 m. ENE of Vannes. Pop. in 1841, 5,277. Cider and butter form its chief articles of commerce.
CARESANA, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 9 m. SSE of Vercelli, near the r. bank of the Sesia.
CAREW, a parish of Pembrokeshire, 4 m. ENE of Pembroke. Pop. in 1841, 1,056.
CARFAN AND PRISK, a township of Cardiganshire, in the p. of Llan-ddewibrefi. Pop. in 1841, 150.
CARGAN, a parish in co. Galway, 3 m. SW of Headford. Area 3,609 acres. Pop. 1,369.
CARGEGHE, a village of the island of Sardinia, in the district and 6 m. SE of Sassari. It contains some mineral springs.

CARGHESE, or CARGESE, a commune of the island of Corsica, in the cant. of Piana, and 15 m. NNW of Ajaccio, on the gulf of Sagone. Pop. 697, consisting of the descendants of the Comene who took refuge in Genoa in 1675, and were established here the following year. This com. is the best cultivated portion of the island, and possesses a port.

CARGILL, a parish in Perthshire, in the district of Strathmore, between the river Tay and the Sidlaw hills, and intersected by the Isla. Pop in 1801, 1,585; in 1841, 1,642. It contains the villages of Burreltown, Wolfhill, and Woodside.
CARGO, a township of Cumberland, in the p. of Stanwix, 3 m. NW of Carlisle, on the Eden, and near the Ship canal. Pop. in 1841, 259.

CARGUAIRAZO, or CARGAVIRAZO, a volcanic mountain of New Grenada, in the district of Chim- CAR borazo, and 12 m. NW of the mountain of that name. It rises above the line of perpetual snow, but grain is successfully cultivated on its declivities. In 1698 the summit was rent by an earthquake, and the surrounding country devastated by the torrents which issued from the chasm.

CARHAIX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Finistere, arrond. of Chateaulin.—The cant. comprises 9 com., and in 1831 contained 14,721 inhabitants.—The town is situated on a lofty hill, near the l. bank of the Hieres, 27 m. E of Chateaulin, and 34 m. NE of Quimper. Pop. in 1841, 2,021. It contains a handsome market-place and a fine promenade, named the Champ-de-Mars; and possesses several tanneries, paper-mills, and manufactories of common hats. Its commerce consists chiefly in drapery and articles of silk manufacture. Fairs are held thrice a-year.—In the environs is the lead-mine of Poullaouen, one of the richest in France; and a subterranean structure supposed to have been an aqueduct of the Gauls, and to whom also the origin of the town is generally to be attributed. La Tour d'Auvergne, whose native place C. was, alleges it to have been the cap. of the Osismii, and the Vorganium or Vorgium of Strabo, Ptolemy, Pomponius Mela, &c., and that it is the locality in which the Bas-Breton is spoken in the greatest purity. The name Carhaix, Keraës, or Kerahes, is by some thought to be derived from Kent-Ahes-locally signifying 'road of Ahes'-two fine roads having been formed by a princess of that name. C. has been the scene of several sanguinary battles.
CARHAM, a parish of Northumberland, on the Tweed, 3 m. S of Coldstream. Area 11,470 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,282.
CARHAMPTON, a parish of Somerset, 1 m. SE of Dunster. Area 6,460 acres. Pop. 682.
CARHUAMAYO, a town of Peru, in the prov. of Junin, 16 m. S of Pasco, on the E side of Chinchaycocha, at an alt. of 13,087 ft. above sea-level.
CARI, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. and 120 m. SSE of Barcelona, on a river of the same name, which runs S, and unites with the Orinoco, 10 m. NW of Angostura.
CARIA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 22 m. SSW of Guarda.
